questions.text: What is the key difference between a ConfigMap and a Secret?

options.text: ConfigMaps can only store strings; Secrets can store binary
Secrets are base64-encoded and intended for sensitive data; ConfigMaps are plain text
ConfigMaps are cluster-scoped; Secrets are namespace-scoped
Secrets are automatically encrypted; ConfigMaps are not

questions.explanation: The main distinction is intent: Secrets are for sensitive data (passwords, keys) and are base64-encoded (but NOT encrypted by default). ConfigMaps are for non-sensitive configuration. Both are namespace-scoped. Secrets get additional protection via RBAC restrictions and optional encryption at rest.

options.text: Mounts the ConfigMap as a volume
Loads all key-value pairs from a ConfigMap as environment variables
References a ConfigMap for use in resource limits
Creates environment variables only for the init container

questions.explanation: envFrom with configMapRef loads all keys from the ConfigMap as environment variables in the container. Each key becomes an env var name and its value becomes the value. Use a prefix with prefix: APP_ to namespace all keys from that ConfigMap.

questions.text: Are Kubernetes Secrets encrypted at rest by default?

options.text: Yes — Kubernetes always encrypts Secrets in etcd
No — they are base64-encoded but stored in plaintext in etcd unless encryption is configured
Yes — using AES-256 automatically
Only in managed Kubernetes services (EKS, GKE, AKS)

questions.explanation: By default, Secrets are stored as base64-encoded plaintext in etcd — NOT encrypted. To encrypt Secrets at rest, configure the API server with an EncryptionConfiguration that uses providers like aescbc or secretbox, or use KMS providers for cloud-managed encryption.

questions.text: What Secret type stores TLS certificate and private key?

options.text: Opaque
kubernetes.io/tls
kubernetes.io/ssl
kubernetes.io/certificate

questions.explanation: kubernetes.io/tls Secrets must contain tls.crt (certificate) and tls.key (private key) keys. These are used by Ingress resources for TLS termination. Create them with: kubectl create secret tls my-tls --cert=cert.pem --key=key.pem

questions.text: If you mount a ConfigMap as a volume, what happens when the ConfigMap is updated?

options.text: The Pod must be restarted to see changes
The mounted files are automatically updated (with a short delay)
The Pod is automatically restarted
Changes are rejected until the Pod is deleted

questions.explanation: When a ConfigMap is mounted as a volume, Kubernetes automatically updates the files when the ConfigMap changes (with a delay of kubelet's sync period + cache TTL). However, applications must be designed to reload config on file changes. Environment variables from ConfigMaps do NOT update automatically.

questions.text: What is the 'stringData' field in a Secret?

options.text: An alternative to 'data' that accepts plain text values (auto-base64-encoded)
String-type data stored without base64 encoding
Data that must be in string format
A deprecated field for legacy compatibility

questions.explanation: stringData allows you to specify Secret values as plain text in your YAML manifests — Kubernetes automatically base64-encodes them when storing. This is more convenient than manually encoding values. Note: kubectl get secret -o yaml will show the data field (base64), not stringData.

questions.text: What is the External Secrets Operator used for?

options.text: Exporting Kubernetes Secrets to external systems
Synchronizing secrets from external stores (Vault, AWS SSM, GCP SM) into Kubernetes Secrets
Encrypting Kubernetes Secrets automatically
Providing external RBAC for Secret access

questions.explanation: The External Secrets Operator (ESO) reads secrets from external secret stores (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault) and creates Kubernetes Secrets. This is the recommended pattern for enterprise environments — secrets are managed externally and synced into the cluster.

questions.text: How do you pass a specific key from a ConfigMap as an environment variable with a different name?

options.text: Use envFrom with a prefix
Use env[].valueFrom.configMapKeyRef with name (CM name) and key (CM key)
Use configMap.keyMapping in the Pod spec
You cannot rename keys from ConfigMaps

questions.explanation: To map a specific ConfigMap key to an env var with a specific name: env: - name: MY_VAR valueFrom: configMapKeyRef: name: my-configmap key: original-key. This gives full control over env var names regardless of ConfigMap key names.

questions.text: What is the maximum size of a ConfigMap or Secret?

options.text: 100 KB
1 MB
10 MB
Unlimited

questions.explanation: Both ConfigMaps and Secrets have a 1 MiB (mebibyte) size limit set by etcd. This prevents large objects from overwhelming etcd. For larger files, use a volume with a PersistentVolumeClaim, a cloud storage bucket, or an object store accessed via init containers.

questions.text: How does immutable: true on a ConfigMap or Secret help performance?

options.text: Prevents updates, making Kubernetes skip watching for changes on kubelets
Makes the data faster to retrieve from etcd
Enables caching in the API server
Allows the object to be stored in memory only

questions.explanation: Setting immutable: true on a ConfigMap or Secret prevents it from being modified and tells the kubelet to stop watching the API server for changes. For clusters with thousands of Pods using ConfigMaps, this significantly reduces API server load since kubelets no longer poll for updates.
